videos.transcript: Bucharest Romania. Often called the Paris of the East. It is one of the biggest cities in Eastern Europe. It is the capital of Romania and home to around 2 million people. Hi, I am Martijn and your guide in Bucharest. After watching this travel guide you know a lot about this city to get the most out of your trip. From this huge building with more than 1100 rooms... ... to the old town and beautiful churches. If this travel guide is giving you new travel tips or maybe you just like it... ...please give a thumbs up or leave a comment under the video. That's the way you can help so other people can discover the video as well and I get to create new videos for you in the future. The city has two airports and the biggest one is "Henri Coandă". It's located 18 kilometers north of the city center. To travel to the city you can use the airport bus the journey takes around 45 minutes and the tickets will cost less than 1 euro. Another way to travel is by train there is a 24-hour service the journey takes around 30 minutes and a ticket costs less than 1,50 euro. The last way to travel is by taxi and the journey costs around 12 euros. Let's start the Bucharest Travel Guide over here in the old city center and the locals call it "Centrul Vechi". It's the old core of the city but with a twist. During the Communist period over 10.000 houses were demolished to build the Palace of the Parliament and later more about that building. This part was spared and became seedy a city part of town. But there was a restoration between 2007 and 2011. Nowadays it's a nice place with old buildings, a lot of restaurants, boutiques and nightclubs. And one of the best places to take pictures is the ‘Umbrella Alley’ located in The "Pasajul Victoria". This is a building you can't miss the Palace of the Parliament. It has a surface of 365.000 square meters. That makes it the largest building in Europe and also the heaviest in the whole world... ...since it weighs over 4 billion kilograms. The palace was built in a period of 13 years and the entire construction is a result of more than 100.000 workers. Today it is the seat of the Romanian Parliament and a major tourist attraction. You can book guided tours in advance and to do that you have to call a phone number. It's really old school. And you can find a number in the text below the video. Do that one day before you want to go and also bring your passport during your visit. Close to the Palace, it's only a 15-minute walk, you will find Union Square. It's one of the biggest and most important in the city. It's well known for The Fountains... ... It's also a major transportation hub for metros and buses and there is always a traffic jam. Just like the whole city. And we are lucky in the evening there are sometimes some light shows. And close to the square you'll find some important landmarks for example the National Museum of Art. That museum is located in an old Royal Palace and houses regular collections of Romanian and European Art. The European art gallery contains work by famous artists such as Rembrandt and Monet... ...and there is also a Romanian art gallery that showcases the work of Romanian artists. Almost next to the museum you will find a historic "Atheneum". The building was inaugurated in 1888 and has since been the home of the "George Enescu Philharmonic Orchestra". it is considered to be one of the most important concert halls in Europe. Due to its excellent acoustics and unique architectural style. In a moment you know more about this beautiful church but first the "Cismigiu Gardens". This public park is located in the city center and it is the oldest and most popular Park in town. It covers an area of approximately 17 hectares. The park was designed in the mid-19th century by the German landscape architect Carl Meyer. 500 meters to the west from the gardens you will find Revolution square and this is an important historic site... ...because this was the heart of the Romanian revolution in 1989. Which led to the overthrown of the communist government. The most prominent feature of the square is the memorial of rebirth a 25 meter tall marble pillar that was erected in 2005. A spot close to the Revolution Square is the "Kretzulescu Church" and that's an old one the construction started in 1720. The church has been restored several times and plans to demolish it were not carried out. Another recommendation to visit is the "Stavropoleos Monastery". It's an old Eastern Orthodox Monastery located in the Heart of the City center. It was built in 1724 by Greek Monk and it's well known as one of the most beautiful and well preserved religious buildings in the city. In a moment you know the biggest park in any capital city in Europe... ...and also a building that will remind you of Paris... ...but first one of the oldest constructions in town the Old Princely Court. It was the residence for centuries for the princess Wallachia (known as Vlad the Impaler, Dracula). It was destroyed in a 19th century by an earthquake and at the moment there is a reconstruction going on... ...so I can't show you the building only this crap. But what I can show you is the old Court Church. Considered as the oldest preserved Church in its original appearance in the city. The old Court church was part of the Princely Court and served for coronation Ceremonies for 200 years. A perfect place to learn more about the history of this country is the national museum of Romanian History. It houses over 750.000 artifacts including potteries, weapons and costumes. The museum was founded in 1972 and it's located in a historic building formerly called the Palace of the posts. The inspiration for this Palace came from the federal post Palace in Geneva Switzerland. A must visit is the "Herăstrau Park". It is the largest park in any capital city in Europe. The park is centered around the Herăstrau lake. A man-made lake that was created in the 1930s as part of a project to improve the city's water supply. In this park you will also find the "Village Museum". This museum showcases the traditional Romanian village life... ...and that includes real-sized historical houses from all regions of the country. When you see this building you might think that you are in Paris but you are not you are still in Bucharest... ...and this is also an Arc Triomphe. It was built in 1935 to remember the Romanian victory in the first world war and to honor The Bravery soldiers. What is the best time for a visit? I think the months April, May, June, September and October. The summers are really hot and the winters are cold and the days in the winter are shorter. This is one of the oldest buildings in town and also one of Europe's final caravanserais. It was built in 1808 and by the middle of the 19th century it was Bucharest's most important commercial complex. Please give a thumbs up when you like this Bucharest Travel Guide... ...and if you don't want to miss new episodes just subscribe on my YouTube channel. It's totally free and it enriches your view of the whole world.

videos.transcript: [Music] This is Bucharest, Europe's most misunderstood capital and quite possibly its most surprising. A city of great contrast where gritty meets gorgeous and every street whispers a story. Bucharest has endured wars, a harsh communist regime and devastating earthquakes that erased many of its historic treasures. Yet beneath the scars lies a vibrant, resilient city bursting with surprises, stories, and soul. This isn't just a travel guide. It's your adventure through 16 unforgettable things to do in Bucharest, one of the Europe's most intriguing cities. Let's dive in. We are kicking things off in the heart of the old town with Stabro Polos Church, a tiny jewel of Romanian Orthodox architecture. Built in 1724 by a Greek monk, this church is one of the best preserved examples of traditional Romanian ecclesiastical architecture and unique blend of Byzantine, Ottoman, and Baro influences. The courtyard is a peaceful oasis filled with cardstone fragments from other demolished churches. Inside you'll find delicate frescos and the sound of Byzantine chants still echoing during services. Coming in at number two, we have the Palace of the Parliament, the heaviest building in the world and the second largest administrative structure after the Pentagon. Commissioned by dictator Nikolai Chaesco in 1984 as the centerpiece of his vision for a new socialist capital, this colossal building contains over 1,000 rooms, although estimates suggest that nearly 60% remain unused even today. Its construction spanned almost a decade and to make a way for the palace a vast section of Bucharest historical town was raised displacing over 40,000 residents and destroying countless architectural treasuries. Just outside the equally ambitious boulevard built to complement the palace stretches out in grand fashion lined with synchronized fonts. If you are planning a visit, check the link in the video description to book your organized tour. You will also find my top recommended day trips from Bucharest, including an unforgettable journey through Transylvania and a visit to the legendary Dracula Castle. At number three on our list is the Patriarchal Hill, the spiritual heart of Romanian Orthodoxy. This historic hilltop has served as the center of the Romanian Orthodox Church since the 17th century. Its crown jewel is the Patriarchal Cathedral completed in 1658. A stunning religious landmark and an important pilgrimage destination especially during Orthodox Easter. But the hill offers more than just the cathedral. It's also home to the patriarchal palace, the official residence of the patriarch, as well as the chapel of St. George entrance is completely free. At number four is Ku Kuber Bucharest most iconic beer hall and a true institution in the heart of a city. Open in 1879, this negotiic masterpiece stunts visitors with its painting ceilings, stained glass windows, and carved woodwork. Over the years, it has hosted everyone from Romanian intellectuals to international celebrities. Yes, it's popular with tourists, but the experience absolutely lives up to the hype. If you are planning to go for dinner, make sure to book ahead on their official website. It gets packed very quickly. Want to enjoy the atmosphere without breaking the bank? Head there for lunch when they offer a daily menu for around €10. A fantastic deal for traditional Romanian food in such a grand setting. Climbing higher at number five is the Dimitri National Village Museum. One of Bucharest top open air attractions. Open in 1936. The museum brings together over 270 authentic buildings from rural Romania from wooden churches and windmant homes and barns. Wandering through its peaceful lanes gives you the rare opportunity to experience the diverse traditions, architecture, and lifestyles of Romanian's countryside, all without ever leaving the capital. Tickets cost around €8 and guided tours are available. At number six, we visit Revolution Square, the historic site where Romania's communist regime fell in a dramatic uprising in December 1989. At its center stands the Rebirth Memorial, a tribute to those who died fighting for freedom. Just behind it is the Royal Palace, now home to the National Museum of Art of Romania, featuring an impressive collection of Romanian and European masterpieces. Next door you'll find the Carol first foundations housed in the former university library with the question statue of Carol first standing proudly in front. Tucked in the corner of the square is the credlesco church a beautifully preserved 18th century Orthodox church which offers a quite contrast to the square turbulent past. Number seven brings us to Liberia Cartures Caruzel. One of the most beautiful bookstores in Europe. Located in a beautifully restored 19th century building in Bucharest old town, this sixth floor, literally heaven, opened in 2015 and quickly became a cultural landmark. Even if you are not book shopping, it's worth a visit just to admire its elegant spiral staircases, whitewash columns, and airy light field interior. Don't miss the top floor cafe. It offers a great view over the entire store. Taking spot eight is the Romanian atom. Open in 1888, this Domio classical concert hall is home to the Gorgan Escoil Ararmonic. The concert hall is known for its outstanding acoustics and circular frescos depicting key moments in Romanian history. Tickets for concerts start around 1015. But even if you don't attend a performance, you can visit the lobby during the day for just €3. Additionally, be sure to take a leisurely stroll around the to soak in the elegant atmosphere of the surrounding area. [Music] At number nine, we step into the living history of Hanul Lu Manuk, the last surviving historic inn of its kind in Bucharest and one of the city's most atmospheric landmarks. Built in 1808, this former travelers lodge once welcomed merchants and diplomats from across the Ottoman Empire. Today, Hanului Manuk is a beloved gathering place functioning as a traditional Romanian restaurant and boutique hotel. Right across the street stands St. Anthony Church, an Orthodox church dedicated to St. Anthony the Great, built in 1816, adding a serene spiritual presence to the bustling historic area. [Music] And speaking of the Arold de Triumph, that's number 10 on our list. Inspired by the famous Ar Triumph in Paris, this landmark was originally built to celebrate Romania's victory in World War I and to honor the bravery of its soldiers. The current stone structure standing proudly at 27 m tall was completed in 1936, replacing earlier temporary wooden versions erected right after the war. Just a short walk from the arch, you'll find the King Michael First Park. A green oasis perfect for a relaxing stroll or picnic. [Music] Number 11 bring us to the St. Nicholas Russian Church. A hidden gem near University Square. Commissioned by Sar Nicholas and completed in 1909. It was built to serve the Russian Orthodox community in Bucharest. Inside you'll find a glowing golden econostasis and richly painted fresco. Entrance is free. [Music] Next we step inside the historic CIC palace. Completed in 1900 by a French architect. This masterpiece was once Romanian central bank. Its glass dome and ornate facade are among the most photographed in the city, reflecting Bucharest strong ties to France, a connection that earned it the nickname Little Paris. While the building isn't always open to the public, guided tours are occasionally available to explore this iconic landmark. Coming in at number 13 is the charming Maka Villa Cross Passage. A cover arcade with a Parisian feel. In the late 19th and early 20th century, French influence shaped in fact much of a city's elegant architecture and culture. Built in the late 19th century to link to busy streets, its yellow stained glass roof and fork layout make it one of Bucharest architectural oddities. Now filled with cafes and shisha lounges, it's a favorite local hangout. Number 14 is for foodies and cafe lovers. Vangot Cafe just off the main street in the old town. Set in a restored 19th century building. The cafe is named after the artist and you'll find his famous paintings and prints all around giving the place a creative galleryike feel. The atmosphere is warm and bohemian, perfect for a slow breakfast or a good coffee break. Just a heads up, while the setting is great, the prices are definitely on the higher side. Number 15 is Victory Avenue, Bucharest, most elegant boulevard. Its southern stretch is scenic and walkable, lined with landmarks, including some we have already mentioned, and stunning architecture. You'll start with Lameita Bitista, a beautifully restored art novel townhouse. And just across from it, it stands the Azai church, an elegant 19th century Orthodox church. A short detour to the west brings you to Victoria Passage, an alley famous for its colorful umbrellas. Continuing along the boulevard, you'll pass the new classical Audon Theater, followed by the Grand Military Circle, also from 1911, currently under a construction. Just beyond is Latari Church, a 17th century gem rebuilt in now style, housing relics of San Cyprien. Before you reach the river, make a stop at the National Museum of Romanian History, housed in a grand neass building. Last but not least, it's worth highlighting a few key buildings scattered around the old town. This include the National Bank of Romania, a powerful symbol of the country's financial legacy. The elegant Palace of Empirka, once home to major cultural institutions, and the grand stock exchange palace, echoing Bucharest commercial past. Together with other historical remnants, they reflect the old town's rich architectural heritage. This wraps up our tour of Bucharest. Honestly, I didn't have high expectation at first, but this city truly surprised me with its rich history, hidden charm, and vibrant culture. Thanks for joining me on this journey through its most unforgettable sites and hidden gems. If you enjoyed this guide, don't forget to like and subscribe for more travel adventures, and be sure to check the link in the video description for the best day trips and recommended tours to make the most of your time in Bucharest. See you on the next adventure. [Music]

If you're wondering "Is Bucharest worth visiting?" the answer is a resounding yes. The city is rich with attractions and activities that cater to diverse interests, ensuring that there are plenty of things to do in Bucharest. 

One of the top things to do in Bucharest is visiting the Palace of Parliament, a colossal structure that stands as a testament to the city's historical and political significance. This building is the second largest administrative building in the world and offers guided tours that provide insights into its grand architecture. For those interested in history and culture, a visit to the Stavropoleos Monastery, with its beautiful frescoes, and the Romanian Athenaeum, a stunning concert hall, are must-see attractions. 

Exploring the Old Town is another highlight of Bucharest travel. This area is bustling with life, featuring cobblestone streets lined with cafes, bars, and shops. It's the perfect spot for enjoying a leisurely stroll or indulging in some local cuisine. Speaking of food, Bucharest is a great place to try traditional Romanian dishes such as sarmale (stuffed cabbage rolls) and mititei (grilled sausages). 

For those seeking unusual things to do in Bucharest, the city offers unique experiences like visiting the Therme București, Europe's largest relaxation center, or exploring the vibrant street art scene in neighborhoods like Pantelimon. Nature lovers can enjoy the tranquil settings of Herăstrău Park or the Botanical Gardens. 

Whether you're interested in history, culture, food, or nightlife, Bucharest has something to offer. With its blend of old-world charm and modern appeal, the city is undoubtedly worth visiting and exploring.

videos.transcript: Bucharest Romania. Frequently referred to as the "Paris of the East." It ranks among the largest cities in Eastern Europe. Serving as the capital of Romania, it is inhabited by approximately 2 million residents. Hello, I'm Alp Galip, and I'll be your guide in Bucharest. By the end of this travel guide, you'll have plenty of insight into the city, helping you make the most of your visit. From historical heritage to shopping, dining, relaxation, instagrammable photo spots and transportation. If this guide has provided you with useful travel tips, or if you simply enjoyed it, please give it a thumbs up or leave a comment below. This is a great way for you to support the channel, helping others find the video, and allowing me to create more content for you in the future. The city is served by two airports, with "Henri Coandă" being the largest. It is situated 18 kilometers to the north of the city center. To reach the city, you can take the airport bus, which takes approximately 45 minutes, and tickets cost under 1 euro. Alternatively, you can travel by train, which operates 24/7; the trip lasts around 30 minutes, and tickets are priced at less than 1.50 euros. Lastly, you can take a taxi or uber, with the fare being around 12 euros for the journey. Let’s start the Bucharest Travel Guide with the famous Centrul Vechi, the Old Town. This area is full of history and charm, with narrow cobblestone streets. You’ll find stunning old buildings and lively cafés everywhere you look. Centrul Vechi is perfect for exploring both day and night. Don’t miss the Stavropoleos Monastery, a true gem of architecture. The Old Princely Court takes you back to the time of Vlad the Impaler. It’s a place where history, culture, and fun come together beautifully. The Palace of the Parliament is an iconic landmark in Bucharest and heaviest buildings in the world. It took 13 years and over 100,000 workers to complete this massive structure. Today, it serves as the home of the Romanian Parliament and is a popular tourist spot. Guided tours are available, but you’ll need to book in advance and bring your passport. Union Square is a major landmark just a 15-minute walk from the Palace. It’s famous for its fountains, evening light shows, and busy atmosphere. As a key transportation hub, it’s always bustling with metros, buses, and traffic. Caru Cu Bere is one of Bucharest's oldest and most iconic restaurants, known for its stunning interior and lively atmosphere. It’s the perfect place to enjoy traditional Romanian food and local beer. Right next to the museum is the historic Atheneum, opened in 1888. Known for its stunning architecture and exceptional acoustics, it hosts the George Enescu Philharmonic Orchestra. Bucharest's parks, like Herăstrău and Cismigiu Gardens, offer peaceful green spaces to relax and explore. They’re perfect for a stroll, a boat ride, or simply enjoying nature in the city. Revolution Square, located 500 meters west of Cismigiu Gardens, is a key historic site. It played a central role in the 1989 Romanian Revolution, which ended communist rule. The square's standout feature is the 25-meter tall marble Memorial of Rebirth, added in 2005. This site holds deep significance for Romania's modern history. Near Revolution Square is the historic Kretzulescu Church, built in 1720. Despite restoration and past demolition plans, the church remains a preserved landmark. The Stavropoleos Monastery is a must-see in the heart of the city center. Built in 1724 by a Greek monk, it’s an Eastern Orthodox treasure. Known for its beauty, it’s one of the best-preserved religious landmarks in Bucharest. The Princely Court Church is the oldest in Bucharest, still in its original form. It was part of the Old Princely Court and hosted coronation ceremonies for 200 years. Casa de Economii și Consemnațiuni or Palace of the Deposits and Consignments, also known as CEC Palace, is a stunning historic building in Bucharest. Its elegant architecture and location makes it one of the city’s most photographed landmarks. The Arcul de Triumf in Bucharest, reminiscent of Arc de Triomphe in Paris, was built in 1935. It commemorates Romania's victory in World War I and honors the bravery of its soldiers. The best time to visit Bucharest is in April, May, June, September, or October. Summers are very hot, while winters are cold with shorter days. Manuc’s Inn is one of Bucharest’s oldest buildings and one of Europe’s last caravanserais, built in 1808. By the mid-19th century, it became the city’s most important commercial hub. Today, it houses the famous Hanul Lui Manuc restaurant, known for its traditional Romanian dishes. The restaurant offers a unique dining experience in a historic setting. The Odeon Theatre, a cultural gem in Bucharest, is known for its elegant architecture and vibrant performances. Nearby, the Atatürk Statue honors the founder of modern Turkey, symbolizing strong Romanian-Turkish ties. Pasajul Macca-Villacrosse is a cozy, covered passage filled with charming cafés and a glass roof. Pasajul Victoria, with its colorful umbrellas, creates a cheerful and Instagram-worthy spot to explore. Therme București is a massive wellness and relaxation center located just outside the city. It features thermal pools, saunas, and lush tropical gardens, making it a perfect escape year-round. Cărturești Carusel is a stunning bookstore in the heart of Bucharest, housed in a beautifully restored 19th-century building. With its elegant spiral staircases and unique design, it’s a paradise for book lovers and photographers alike. Băneasa Shopping City is a top shopping destination with a variety of international brands and dining options. It also features entertainment facilities, making it perfect for a fun day out. Bucharest's Christmas markets, like those in Constitution Square and West Side, are filled with festive charm, local crafts, and seasonal treats. They create a magical holiday atmosphere perfect for celebrating winter in the city. Bucharest transforms into a festive wonderland during the holiday season, with sparkling lights and decorations throughout the city. The lively atmosphere is perfect for enjoying Christmas markets, music, and seasonal events. Bucharest’s nightlife is vibrant, with a mix of trendy clubs, cozy bars, and live music venues. The Old Town is the hotspot, offering something for everyone to enjoy after dark. Romanian cuisine is hearty and flavorful, with delicious options to try. Bean soup served in a bread bowl, beef soup with lovage, and lamb soup are must-try traditional dishes. For meat lovers, Romanian steaks are a treat with their rich seasoning. Don’t miss papanași , a sweet dessert of fried or boiled dough topped with sour cream and jam. Day tours from Bucharest are perfect for exploring Romania’s stunning landmarks like Castelul Peleş , a fairy-tale residence, and the legendary Castelul Bran. You can also visit the charming medieval city of Brașov , nestled in the Carpathian Mountains. If you enjoyed this Bucharest Travel Guide, give it a thumbs up! Subscribe to my YouTube channel for free to catch all the new episodes. It’s a great way to expand your perspective on the world.
